name: FR La Batailleuse
events: 1 Mar 1942 HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and sank the Vichy-French transport P.L.M. 20 (5417 GRT, built 1920) about 5 nautical miles east of Mehedia, Tunisia in position 35°26'N, 11°09'E.

This was French convoy S 14 consisting of the above mentioned P.L.M. 20 as well as the transports Château Latour (1912 GRT, built 1916), Sainte Odette (4140 GRT, built 1938) and Sainte Simone (5535 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boat Bombarde and the minesweeping sloop La Batailleuse.

At the time of the attack, La Batailleuse and Sainte Simone were trailing behind as the latter could only make 5 knots. Bombarde dropped five depth charges but without causing damage to the attacker. La Batailleuse arrived on the scene and claimed to have been missed by a torpedo but this is not substantiated by HMS Unbeaten’s patrol report. P.L.M. 20 sank in less than two minutes, nine were killed or missing, thirty-two survivors were picked up.

0340 B/1 - Sighted a large French trawler trawling south of Kuriat with her lights on. Patrolled in position 090° - Mahedia lighthouse - 5 miles during the day. Investigated the wreck of a modern Italian transport of the Andrea Gritti-class.

[No time given in the patrol report] Sighted a convoy of one tanker (P.L.M. 20 looked like a tanker, engine and funnel aft and the bridge amidships) of about 7000 tons and two merchant vessels of 6000 and 5000 tons. They were escorted by at least one but probably more destroyers. Started attack.
1938 B/1 - Fired four torpedoes at the tanker from 4000 yards. One hit was obtained.
1951 B/1 - A counter attack followed by what appeared to be two destroyers. Seven depth charges were dropped fairly close. Later eight more depth charges were dropped but these were not close.

2025 B/1 - Returned to periscope depth. Observed three or four vessels with steaming lights on, one facing south, the others north. A destroyer was signalling to the Citadel on Cape Mahedia which was answering.

2032 B/1 - Eight more depth charges were dropped. By now the battery was very low so Lt. Martin decided to retire to seaward to charge and return in the morning to investigate the area where the attack had taken place. At 2336 B/1, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1203 B/1 was received giving instructions on leaving patrol. (1)
14 Oct 1942 At 2235 A/14, south of Kuriat Island, in position 35°42'N, 11°00'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted three southbound lighted ships. She closed to attack.
At 2238 A/14, HE was heard to the eastward and the attack on the lighted ships, which were thought to be most likely Vichy-French, was broken off and course was altered to the east.
At 2242 A/14, the source of the HE was not in sight but from bearings of the HE the enemy was northbound so the attack was shifted again to the lighted ships.

These consisted of a small ship, probably a destroyer or torpedo boat which was also burning navigation lights, and two fairly large transports brilliantly illuminated. They were inside territorial waters. It was not possible to get inside 4000 yards or close enough to verify what the markings on their sides were. It seemed possible these were hospital ships though and torpedoes were therefore not fired and the attack was broken off at 2340 A/14.

Possibly the convoy sighted was the one made up of the Vichy French tranports Rabelais (4999 GRT, built 1921) and Oasis (727 GRT, built 1938) escorted by the minesweeping sloop La Batailleuse. This convoy was expected of Kerkennah Buoy No.2 at 0800 A/25. [See file DEFE3/852/p. 857]. (2)
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
